perturbation.original_text: (a) Receiving Party shall not disclose any Confidential Information to third parties for five (5) years following the date of its disclosure by Disclosing Party to Receiving Party, except to Receiving Party's consultants as provided below.
perturbation.changed_text: (a) Receiving Party shall not disclose any Confidential Information to third parties for five (5) years following the date of its disclosure by Disclosing Party to Receiving Party, and is forbidden to disclose such information to Receiving Party's consultants.
perturbation.explanation: By forbidding disclosure to consultants, this modification directly contradicts the original clause that allows disclosure to consultants, thus creating an ambiguity and hindering enforcement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Washington law requires clarity in contract terms. The contradiction between allowing and forbidding disclosure to consultants creates an ambiguity that makes the contract vulnerable to legal challenge. This ambiguity contradicts the fundamental principles of contract law, making enforcement difficult. It will be difficult to show breach of contract with such conflicting language.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by explicitly forbidding disclosure to consultants, which directly clashes with the original text allowing it. This alteration introduces an ambiguity regarding the Receiving Party's obligations, making it difficult to enforce the confidentiality agreement. The law explanation supports this by stating that Washington law requires clarity in contract terms, and the contradiction between allowing and forbidding disclosure makes the contract vulnerable to legal challenge. Scraped Law Snippet 1 alludes to errors on the Washington State Courts website, suggesting the importance of accurate and clear legal documentation, and further reinforcing the need for unambiguous contracts.

perturbation.original_text: (b) Receiving Party shall take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, to keep confidential the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Receiving Party shall take minimal security precautions, regardless of the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, to keep confidential the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This clause originally requires 'reasonable security precautions,' but the modified clause changes this to 'minimal security precautions,' regardless of the Receiving Party's practices. This contradiction creates ambiguity and uncertainty about the standard of care required.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty of Care under Washington Law
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing "reasonable" to "minimal", there is now no required duty of care, or a standard for that duty of care to be measured by. Under Washington state law, a duty of care is usually required when handling sensitive or private information, and this duty is to be 'reasonable'.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: CACI No. 401. Basic Standard of Care :: California Civil Jury Instructions (CACI) (2025) :: Justia CACI No. 401. Basic Standard of Care Judicial Council of California Civil Jury Instructions (2025 edition) Download PDF 401 . Basic Standard of Care Negligence is the failure to use r easonable care to pr event harm to oneself or to others. A person can be negligent by acting or by failing to act. A person is negligent if that person does something that a reasonably car eful person would not do in the same situation or fails to do something that a reasonably car eful person would do in the same situation. Y ou must decide how a reasonably car eful person would have acted in [ name of plaintiff/defendant ]’ s situation. New September 2003; Revised May 2020 Sources and Authority • “The formulation of the standard of care is a question of law for the court. Once the court has formulated the standard, its application to the facts of the case is a task for the trier of fact if reasonable minds might dif fer as to whether a party’ s conduct has conformed to the standard.” ( Ramir ez v . Plough, Inc (1993) 6 Cal.4th 539, 546 [25 Cal.Rptr .2d 97, 863 P .2d 167], internal citations omitted.) • Restatement Second of T orts, section 282, defines negligence as “conduct which falls below the standard established by law for the protection of others against unreasonable risk of harm.” • Restatement Second of T orts, section 283, provides: “Unless the actor is a child, the standard of conduct to which he must conform to avoid being negligent is that of a reasonable man under like circumstances.” • The California Supreme Court has stated: “Because application of [due care] is inherently situational, the amount of care deemed reasonable in any particular case will vary , while at the same time the standard of conduct itself remains constant, i.e., due care commensurate with the risk posed by the conduct taking into consideration all relevant circumstances. [Citations].” ( Flowers v . T orrance Memorial Hospital Medical Center (1994) 8 Cal.4th 992, 997 [35 Cal.Rptr .2d 685, 884 P .2d 142]; see also T ucker v . Lombardo (1956) 47 Cal.2d 457, 464 [303 P .2d 1041].) • The proper conduct of a reasonable person in a particular situation may become settled by judicial decision or may be established by statute or administrative regulation. ( Ramir ez, supra, 6 Cal.4th at p. 547.) (See CACI Nos. 418 to 421 on negligence per se.) • Negligence can be found in the doing of an act, as well as in the failure to do an act. (Rest.2d T orts, § 284.) 237 Secondary Sources 6 W itkin, Summary of California Law (1 1th ed. 2017) T orts, §§ 998, 999 California T ort Guide (Cont.Ed.Bar 3d ed.) § 1.3 1 Levy et al., California T orts, Ch. 1, Negligence: Duty and Breach , §§ 1.01, 1.02, 1.30 (Matthew Bender) 33 California Forms of Pleading and Practice, Ch. 380, Negligence (Matthew Bender) 16 California Points and Authorities, Ch. 165, Negligence , § 165.31 (Matthew Bender) CACI No. 401 NEGLIGENCE 238 © Judicial Council of California . Page last reviewed April 2025 Today on Verdict The Roberts Court Puts a Velvet Glove on the Iron Fist of Anti-Trans Backlash Cornell Law professor Michael C. perturbation.justification: The change from 'reasonable security precautions' to 'minimal security precautions' directly contradicts the standard of care expected in handling sensitive information. Scraped Law Snippet 2, CACI No. 401, defines negligence as 'the failure to use reasonable care to prevent harm.' By mandating only 'minimal' precautions, the changed text falls below the legally required standard of 'reasonable care,' thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (c) Any software and documentation provided under this Agreement is provided with RESTRICTED RIGHTS. Use, duplication, or disclosure by the Government is subject to restrictions as set forth in subparagraph (c)(1)(ii) of The Rights in Technical Data and Computer Software clause at DFARS 252.227-7013 or subparagraphs (c)(1) and (2) of the Commercial Computer Software -- Restricted Rights at 48 CFR 52.227-19, as applicable.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) Any software and documentation provided under this Agreement is provided with UNRESTRICTED RIGHTS. Use, duplication, or disclosure by the Government is not subject to any restrictions.
perturbation.explanation: By stating 'UNRESTRICTED RIGHTS' and that the government is not subject to any restrictions, this directly contradicts the original clause, which refers to specific restrictions outlined in DFARS 252.227-7013 and 48 CFR 52.227-19. This creates a direct conflict that affects the legal interpretation of software usage rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) and Defense Federal Acquisition Regulation Supplement (DFARS)
perturbation.law_explanation: 48 CFR § 52.227-19 defines the restricted rights for commercial computer software provided to the government. Stating that the software has 'UNRESTRICTED RIGHTS' and the government is not subject to restrictions contradicts this regulation, potentially voiding the intended protections for the software provider.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 48 CFR § 52.227-19 - Commercial Computer Software License. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 48 CFR § 52.227-19 - Commercial Computer Software License. CFR prev | next 52.227-19 Commercial Computer Software License. As prescribed in 27.409(g) , insert the following clause: Commercial Computer Software License (DEC 2007) (a) Notwithstanding any contrary provisions contained in the Contractor's standard commercial license or lease agreement, the Contractor agrees that the Government will have the rights that are set forth in paragraph (b) of this clause to use, duplicate or disclose any commercial computer software delivered under this contract . The terms and provisions of this contract shall comply with Federal laws and the Federal Acquisition Regulation. (b) (1) The commercial computer software delivered under this contract may not be used, reproduced, or disclosed by the Government except as provided in paragraph (b)(2) of this clause or as expressly stated otherwise in this contract . (2) The commercial computer software may be— (i) Used or copied for use with the computer(s) for which it was acquired, including use at any Government installation to which the computer(s) may be transferred; (ii) Used or copied for use with a backup computer if any computer for which it was acquired is inoperative; (iii) Reproduced for safekeeping (archives) or backup purposes; (iv) Modified, adapted, or combined with other computer software , provided that the modified, adapted, or combined portions of the derivative software incorporating any of the delivered, commercial computer software shall be subject to same restrictions set forth in this contract ; (v) Disclosed to and reproduced for use by support service Contractors or their subcontractors, subject to the same restrictions set forth in this contract ; and (vi) Used or copied for use with a replacement computer. (3) If the commerc
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts 48 CFR § 52.227-19, which defines the rights the government has to use commercial computer software. The original text acknowledges 'RESTRICTED RIGHTS' per DFARS 252.227-7013 and 48 CFR 52.227-19, whereas the changed text grants 'UNRESTRICTED RIGHTS', stating the government is not subject to any restrictions. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states in 52.227-19 (b)(1): 'The commercial computer software delivered under this contract may not be used, reproduced, or disclosed by the Government except as provided in paragraph (b)(2) of this clause or as expressly stated otherwise in this contract .' This directly conflicts with the changed text's assertion of 'UNRESTRICTED RIGHTS'.

perturbation.original_text: (g) If either party employs attorneys to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys' fees.
perturbation.changed_text: (g) If either party employs attorneys to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, each party shall bear its own attorneys' fees regardless of the outcome.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allows the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees. The modified clause explicitly states that each party bears its own fees, directly contradicting the original term and creating uncertainty about cost recovery in case of legal disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: While RCW 4.84.330 generally allows for attorney's fees in contract cases when the contract provides for it, the modified clause directly negates any entitlement to recover such fees, which removes a right the original text provides. This introduces a clear contradiction in terms.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 4.84.330: Actions on contract or lease which provides that attorneys' fees and costs incurred to enforce provisions be awarded to one of parties—Prevailing party entitled to attorneys' fees—Waiver prohibited. Print RCWs > Title 4 > Chapter 4.84 > Section 4.84.330 4.84.320 << 4.84.330 >> 4.84.340 PDF RCW 4.84.330 Actions on contract or lease which provides that attorneys' fees and costs incurred to enforce provisions be awarded to one of parties — Prevailing party entitled to attorneys' fees — Waiver prohibited. In any action on a contract or lease entered into after September 21, 1977, where such contract or lease specifically provides that attorneys' fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce the provisions of such contract or lease, shall be awarded to one of the parties, the prevailing party, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or lease or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys' fees in addition to costs and necessary disbursements. Attorneys' fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract or lease which is entered into after September 21, 1977. Any provision in any such contract or lease which provides for a waiver of attorneys' fees is void. As used in this section "prevailing party" means the party in whose favor final judgment is rendered. [ 2011 c 336 s 131 ; 1977 ex.s. c 203 s 1 .]

Any provision in any such contract or lease which provides for a waiver of attorneys' fees is void. As used in this section "prevailing party" means the party in whose favor fin
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original text and RCW 4.84.330. The original clause allows the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees, aligning with the statute's intent to award fees where the contract provides for it. The changed text eliminates this possibility, stating each party bears its own fees, which effectively waives the right to recover attorney's fees. RCW 4.84.330 states, "Attorneys' fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract or lease which is entered into after September 21, 1977. Any provision in any such contract or lease which provides for a waiver of attorneys' fees is void." The changed text creates a provision that acts as a waiver, which the statute explicitly prohibits and deems void.
